1: The superscription places Psalm 56 in the historical narrative of David's capture by the Philistines at Gath (1 Sam 21:10-15), where he feigned madness before Achish to escape. יוֹנַת אֵלֶם רְחֹקִים (yônat ʾêlem rĕḥōqîm, "Dove of the Distant Oaks" or "Dove of the Distant Silences") — the tune title: yônāh (dove); ʾêlem: the silent/dumb ones, or the oaks (ambiguous in Hebrew); rĕḥōqîm: the distant/faraway ones. The tune evokes separation and longing. מִכְתָּם (miktām): a poem-type whose exact meaning is uncertain — possibly "golden poem" or "writing of David." חָנֵּנִי אֱלֹהִים כִּי-שְׁאָפַנִי אֱנוֹשׁ (ḥonnênî ʾĕlōhîm kî-shĕʾāpanî ʾĕnôsh, "Be merciful to me, O God, for men are trampling on me") — shāʾap: to trample, to pant after (as a predator pants after prey) — the sense of being relentlessly hunted.
2: שֹׁאֲפַי כָּל-הַיּוֹם (shōʾăpay kol-hayyôm, "my enemies trample on me all day long") — shāʾap (piel: to pant after, to trample): the continuous relentless assault — no pause, no reprieve. כִּי-רַבִּים לֹחֲמִים לִי מָרוֹם (kî-rabbîm lōḥămîm lî māhôm, "for many are arrayed against me proudly") — māhôm: from on high, proudly (or: "with arrogance"). The numerical and attitudinal disparity: many against one, and they are proud of it.
3: יוֹם אִירָא אֲנִי אֵלֶיךָ אֶבְטָח (yôm ʾîrāʾ ʾănî ʾêlêkā ʾebṭāḥ, "When I am afraid, I will put my trust in you") — the pattern of the Psalter: fear acknowledged and then redirected. ʾĀrêʾ (qal imperfect/jussive: "when I am afraid" — a conditional): the fear is real and not denied. Bāṭaḥ: the pivot — at the moment of fear, the action of trust begins. The verse is a compressed theology of faith: not the absence of fear but the act of trust amid fear.
4: בֵּאלֹהִים אֲהַלֵּל דְּבָרוֹ (bêʾlōhîm ʾăhallêl dĕbārô, "In God — whose word I praise") — dābār: the divine word (dēbār) as the object of praise. The word "I praise" God's word even before the deliverance comes — praising the promise as if it were already fulfilled. בֵּאלֹהִים בָּטַחְתִּי לֹא אִירָא (bêʾlōhîm bāṭaḥtî lōʾ ʾîrāʾ, "in God I trust; I will not be afraid") — the trust eliminates the fear. מַה-יַּעֲשֶׂה בָשָׂר לִי (māh-yaʿăśeh bāśār lî, "what can a mere mortal do to me?") — bāśār: flesh — the word for mortal, perishable humanity. The contrast: God (eternal, powerful) against bāśār (transient flesh). Paul echoes this logic in Rom 8:31: "If God is for us, who can be against us?"
5: כָּל-הַיּוֹם דְּבָרַי יְעַצֵּבוּ (kol-hayyôm dĕbāray yĕʿaṣṣêbû, "All day long they distort my words") — ʿāṣab (piel: to pain, to hurt — here: to twist, to torture words): the enemies take the Psalmist's speech and deform it into something harmful. עָלַי כָּל-מַחְשְׁבֹתָם לְרָעָה (ʿālay kol-maḥshĕbōtām lĕrāʿāh, "all their scheming against me is for evil") — ḥāshab: to reckon, to devise — the deliberate calculation of evil (the accounting word of Ps 32:2, now applied to malicious planning).
6: יָגוּרוּ יִצְפֹּנוּ הֵמָּה עֲקֵבַי יִשְׁמֹרוּ (yāgûrû yiṣpōnû hêmmāh ʿăqêbay yishmōrû, "They gather themselves to stir up trouble, they lie in ambush, they track my steps") — gûr: to sojourn/to stir (here: to conspire together); ṣāpan (qal: to hide, to lie in ambush); ʿāqēb: heel (tracking step by step — literally watching the heels as a stalker follows the last visible footfall). כַּאֲשֶׁר קִוּוּ נַפְשִׁי (kaʾăsher qiwwû napshî, "as they wait to take my life") — qāwāh (piel: to wait for, to lie in wait): the predator's patient anticipation of the fatal moment.
7: עַל-אָוֶן פַּלֶּט-לָמוֹ (ʿal-ʾāwen palleṭ-lāmô, "For their crime, should they escape?") — the rhetorical question of justice: the implied answer is no. בְּאַף עַמִּים הוֹרֵד אֱלֹהִים (bĕʾap ʿammîm hôrêd ʾĕlōhîm, "In wrath cast down the peoples, O God!") — ʾap: the nostril/anger of God (the heated breath of divine wrath); yārad (hiphil: to bring down, to cast down): the nations brought low in divine anger. The Psalmist appeals to the standard of divine justice against all wickedness, not merely personal vengeance.
8: נֹדִי סָפַרְתָּה אָתָּה (nōdî sāpartāh ʾāttāh, "You have recorded my restless wandering") — nôd: wandering, restless movement (the same root as nādad — to flee, to wander in exile). Sāpar: to count, to number, to record. שִׂימָה דִמְעָתִי בְנֹאדֶךָ (śîmāh dimʿātî bĕnōʾdekā, "store my tears in your wineskin") — nōʾd: a wineskin/leather bottle — crucially, the same root as nôd (wandering). The Hebrew plays on the identity: my wandering (nōdî) → your wineskin (nōʾdekā). God stores the wandering in a bottle of wandering — the tears of exile kept in the vessel of exile itself. הֲלֹא בְּסִפְרָתֶךָ (hălōʾ bĕsiprātekā, "are they not written in your book?") — God's sêper (book/scroll): the divine record of each tear, each displacement. Compare the book of remembrance in Mal 3:16.
9: אָז יָשׁוּבוּ אוֹיְבַי אָחוֹר (ʾāz yāshûbû ʾôyĕbay ʾāḥôr, "On the day I call out, my enemies will turn back") — shûb ʾāḥôr: to turn back, to retreat (the reversal of the enemy's advance). בְּיוֹם אֶקְרָא (bĕyôm ʾeqrāʾ): the decisive "day of calling" — when the Psalmist cries out, the enemy turns. The causative link: the prayer itself is the force that reverses the enemy's momentum. זֶה-יָדַעְתִּי כִּי-אֱלֹהִים לִי (zeh-yādaʿtî kî-ʾĕlōhîm lî, "this I know: God is on my side") — lî: for me, on my side. The confident yādaʿtî (I know — the covenantal knowledge): the certainty is grounded not in circumstances but in the character of God.
10: בֵּאלֹהִים אֲהַלֵּל דָּבָר (bêʾlōhîm ʾăhallêl dābār, "In God — whose word I praise") — the first half of the refrain from v4. בַּיהוָה אֲהַלֵּל דָּבָר (baYHWH ʾăhallêl dābār, "in the LORD — whose word I praise") — the refrain doubled with the covenant name YHWH added: in v4, the divine name was ʾĕlōhîm; here both names appear. The word (dābār) of both God in his power and YHWH in his covenant relationship is praised — the divine speech is both universal and covenantally particular.
11: בֵּאלֹהִים בָּטַחְתִּי לֹא אִירָא (bêʾlōhîm bāṭaḥtî lōʾ ʾîrāʾ, "In God I trust; I will not be afraid") — the refrain of v4 restated: trust eliminates fear. מַה-יַּעֲשֶׂה אָדָם לִי (māh-yaʿăśeh ʾādām lî, "What can human beings do to me?") — in v4 it was bāśār (flesh); here it is ʾādām (human being). The two words for human mortality and finitude bracket the enemy's power: what can flesh (bāśār) do? What can a human (ʾādām) do? The God who transcends both renders both powerless.
12: עָלַי אֱלֹהִים נְדָרֶיךָ (ʿālay ʾĕlōhîm nĕdārêkā, "I have vows upon me to you, O God") — neder: a vow (the binding promise made to God in a crisis, to be fulfilled upon deliverance — Ps 50:14; Jonah 2:9). אֲשַׁלֵּם תּוֹדֹת לָךְ (ʾăshallêm tôdōt lāk, "I will fulfill them; I will offer you my thanksgiving") — shālam (piel: to complete, to pay in full — the wholeness-root); tôdāh: thanksgiving, the thank-offering (the sacrifice of praise — Lev 7:12-15).
13: כִּי הִצַּלְתָּ נַפְשִׁי מִמָּוֶת (kî hiṣṣaltā napshî mimmāwet, "For you have delivered my soul from death") — nāṣal (hiphil: to snatch away, to rescue — as from the jaws of a predator): the rescue is a snatching, a sudden saving movement. הֲלֹא רַגְלַי מִדֶּחִי (hălōʾ raglî middĕḥî, "yes, my feet from stumbling") — deḥî: stumbling, a slip (from dāḥāh: to push, to trip): the foot rescued before the fatal fall. לְהִתְהַלֵּךְ לִפְנֵי אֱלֹהִים בְּאוֹר הַחַיִּים (lĕhithallêk lipnê ʾĕlōhîm bĕʾôr haḥayyîm, "so that I may walk before God in the light of the living") — ʾôr haḥayyîm: "the light of the living" — life as light, death as darkness; to live before God is to walk in the divine luminosity that belongs to the living (contrast Job 3:16 where the stillborn "never see the light"). The rescue from death is the rescue into light.
